{{draft}} {{infobox shinto shrine|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} [[{{PAGENAME}}]] is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in [[Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=Suda Hachiman-jinja Shrine｜Wakayama area｜Sightseeing｜Tourist information |url=https://katsuragisyugen-nihonisan.com/en/tourism/cat01/wakayama/post_28.php |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=Japan Heritage: Katsuragi Shugen—The birthplace of Shugendo, a tradition passed down through generations of believers and villagers |language=en}}</ref>. It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Suda Hachiman Shrine{{!}}Walk the Nankaido |url=https://kinokawa-ryuiki.com/en/spot/suda-hachiman-shrine/ |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=kinokawa-ryuiki.com}}</ref> [[ja:隅田八幡神社]]

It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Suda Hachiman Shrine{{!}}Walk the Nankaido |url=https://kinokawa-ryuiki.com/en/spot/suda-hachiman-shrine/ |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=kinokawa-ryuiki.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=https://webcache.googleusercontent.com/search?q=cache:3ekfFZfKcywJ:www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf&hl=en&gl=ca |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=webcache.googleusercontent.com}}</ref> [[ja:隅田八幡神社]]

{{draft}} {{infobox shinto shrine|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} [[{{PAGENAME}}]] is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in [[Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=Suda Hachiman-jinja Shrine｜Wakayama area｜Sightseeing｜Tourist information |url=https://katsuragisyugen-nihonisan.com/en/tourism/cat01/wakayama/post_28.php |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=Japan Heritage: Katsuragi Shugen—The birthplace of Shugendo, a tradition passed down through generations of believers and villagers |language=en}}</ref>. It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Suda Hachiman Shrine{{!}}Walk the Nankaido |url=https://kinokawa-ryuiki.com/en/spot/suda-hachiman-shrine/ |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=kinokawa-ryuiki.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=https://webcache.googleusercontent.com/search?q=cache:3ekfFZfKcywJ:www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf&hl=en&gl=ca |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=webcache.googleusercontent.com}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref> [[ja:隅田八幡神社]]

It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Suda Hachiman Shrine{{!}}Walk the Nankaido |url=https://kinokawa-ryuiki.com/en/spot/suda-hachiman-shrine/ |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=kinokawa-ryuiki.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=https://webcache.googleusercontent.com/search?q=cache:3ekfFZfKcywJ:www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf&hl=en&gl=ca |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=webcache.googleusercontent.com}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref> [[ja:隅田八幡神社]]

{{draft}} {{infobox shinto shrine|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} [[{{PAGENAME}}]] is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in [[Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=Suda Hachiman-jinja Shrine｜Wakayama area｜Sightseeing｜Tourist information |url=https://katsuragisyugen-nihonisan.com/en/tourism/cat01/wakayama/post_28.php |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=Japan Heritage: Katsuragi Shugen—The birthplace of Shugendo, a tradition passed down through generations of believers and villagers |language=en}}</ref>. It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Suda Hachiman Shrine{{!}}Walk the Nankaido |url=https://kinokawa-ryuiki.com/en/spot/suda-hachiman-shrine/ |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=kinokawa-ryuiki.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=https://webcache.googleusercontent.com/search?q=cache:3ekfFZfKcywJ:www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf&hl=en&gl=ca |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=webcache.googleusercontent.com}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was erected on the Suda no Shõ estate, denoting its notable roots in land ownership and religious networks during its founding period.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:隅田八幡神社]]
==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] [[Category:Hachimangu]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakayama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] [[Category:859 establishments]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] }}

{{draft}} {{infobox shinto shrine|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} [[{{PAGENAME}}]] is a [[Shinto Shrine]] in [[Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=Suda Hachiman-jinja Shrine｜Wakayama area｜Sightseeing｜Tourist information |url=https://katsuragisyugen-nihonisan.com/en/tourism/cat01/wakayama/post_28.php |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=Japan Heritage: Katsuragi Shugen—The birthplace of Shugendo, a tradition passed down through generations of believers and villagers |language=en}}</ref>. It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Suda Hachiman Shrine{{!}}Walk the Nankaido |url=https://kinokawa-ryuiki.com/en/spot/suda-hachiman-shrine/ |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=kinokawa-ryuiki.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=https://webcache.googleusercontent.com/search?q=cache:3ekfFZfKcywJ:www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf&hl=en&gl=ca |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=webcache.googleusercontent.com}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:隅田八幡神社]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{infobox shinto shrine|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} '''Suda Hachiman Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bentley |first=John R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uaoLEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PT24&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&hl=en |title=The Birth of Japanese Historiography |date=2020-12-28 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-29569-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wd5IAQAAIAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&q=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&hl=en |title=Impressions: Official Publication of the Ukiyo-e Society of America |date=2009 |publisher=Ukiyo-e Society of America |language=en}}</ref> formerly [[Kii Province]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Farris |first=William Wayne |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SYOUEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA48&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&hl=en |title=A Bowl for a Coin: A Commodity History of Japanese Tea |date=2019 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8261-7 |language=en}}</ref> It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bronze Mirror of Suda Hachiman Jinja Shrine {{!}} Search Details |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R1-00643.html |access-date=2023-11-22 |website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=https://webcache.googleusercontent.com/search?q=cache:3ekfFZfKcywJ:www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf&hl=en&gl=ca |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=webcache.googleusercontent.com}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />
==References== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakayama Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] [[Category:859 establishments]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]]
{{Hachiman Faith}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} '''Suda Hachiman Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bentley |first=John R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uaoLEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PT24 |title=The Birth of Japanese Historiography |date=2020-12-28 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-29569-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wd5IAQAAIAAJ&q=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine |title=Impressions: Official Publication of the Ukiyo-e Society of America |date=2009 |publisher=Ukiyo-e Society of America |language=en}}</ref> formerly [[Kii Province]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Farris |first=William Wayne |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SYOUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PA48 |title=A Bowl for a Coin: A Commodity History of Japanese Tea |date=2019 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8261-7 |language=en}}</ref> It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bronze Mirror of Suda Hachiman Jinja Shrine {{!}} Search Details |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R1-00643.html |access-date=2023-11-22 |website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=www.columbia.edu}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |jstor=42597980 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />
==References== {{reflist}}
{{Hachiman Faith}}

Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} '''Suda Hachiman Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bentley |first=John R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uaoLEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PT24 |title=The Birth of Japanese Historiography |date=2020-12-28 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-29569-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wd5IAQAAIAAJ&q=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine |title=Impressions: Official Publication of the Ukiyo-e Society of America |date=2009 |publisher=Ukiyo-e Society of America |language=en}}</ref> formerly [[Kii Province]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Farris |first=William Wayne |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SYOUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PA48 |title=A Bowl for a Coin: A Commodity History of Japanese Tea |date=2019 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8261-7 |language=en}}</ref> It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bronze Mirror of Suda Hachiman Jinja Shrine {{!}} Search Details |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R1-00643.html |access-date=2023-11-22 |website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=www.columbia.edu}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |jstor=42597980 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} '''Suda Hachiman Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bentley |first=John R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uaoLEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PT24 |title=The Birth of Japanese Historiography |date=2020-12-28 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-29569-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wd5IAQAAIAAJ&q=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine |title=Impressions: Official Publication of the Ukiyo-e Society of America |date=2009 |publisher=Ukiyo-e Society of America |language=en}}</ref> formerly [[Kii Province]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Farris |first=William Wayne |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SYOUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PA48 |title=A Bowl for a Coin: A Commodity History of Japanese Tea |date=2019 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8261-7 |language=en}}</ref> It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bronze Mirror of Suda Hachiman Jinja Shrine {{!}} Search Details |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R1-00643.html |access-date=2023-11-22 |website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=www.columbia.edu}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |jstor=42597980 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} '''Suda Hachiman Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bentley |first=John R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uaoLEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PT24 |title=The Birth of Japanese Historiography |date=2020-12-28 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-29569-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wd5IAQAAIAAJ&q=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine |title=Impressions: Official Publication of the Ukiyo-e Society of America |date=2009 |publisher=Ukiyo-e Society of America |language=en}}</ref> formerly [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Farris |first=William Wayne |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SYOUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PA48 |title=A Bowl for a Coin: A Commodity History of Japanese Tea |date=2019 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8261-7 |language=en}}</ref> It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bronze Mirror of Suda Hachiman Jinja Shrine {{!}} Search Details |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R1-00643.html |access-date=2023-11-22 |website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=www.columbia.edu}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |jstor=42597980 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suda-hachiman-jinja]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine]][[ja:隅田八幡神社]][[ko:스다하치만 신사]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10961321]] Wikidata name: Suda Hachiman Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} '''Suda Hachiman Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bentley |first=John R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uaoLEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PT24 |title=The Birth of Japanese Historiography |date=2020-12-28 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-29569-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wd5IAQAAIAAJ&q=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine |title=Impressions: Official Publication of the Ukiyo-e Society of America |date=2009 |publisher=Ukiyo-e Society of America |language=en}}</ref> formerly [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Farris |first=William Wayne |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SYOUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PA48 |title=A Bowl for a Coin: A Commodity History of Japanese Tea |date=2019 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8261-7 |language=en}}</ref> It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bronze Mirror of Suda Hachiman Jinja Shrine {{!}} Search Details |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R1-00643.html |access-date=2023-11-22 |website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=www.columbia.edu}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |jstor=42597980 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Suda-hachiman-jinja]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine]][[ja:隅田八幡神社]][[ko:스다하치만 신사]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suda-hachiman-jinja]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine]][[ja:隅田八幡神社]][[ko:스다하치만 신사]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10961321]] Wikidata name: Suda Hachiman Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} '''Suda Hachiman Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bentley |first=John R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uaoLEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PT24 |title=The Birth of Japanese Historiography |date=2020-12-28 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-29569-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wd5IAQAAIAAJ&q=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine |title=Impressions: Official Publication of the Ukiyo-e Society of America |date=2009 |publisher=Ukiyo-e Society of America |language=en}}</ref> formerly [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Farris |first=William Wayne |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SYOUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PA48 |title=A Bowl for a Coin: A Commodity History of Japanese Tea |date=2019 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8261-7 |language=en}}</ref> It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bronze Mirror of Suda Hachiman Jinja Shrine {{!}} Search Details |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R1-00643.html |access-date=2023-11-22 |website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=www.columbia.edu}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |jstor=42597980 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} '''Suda Hachiman Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bentley |first=John R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uaoLEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PT24 |title=The Birth of Japanese Historiography |date=2020-12-28 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-29569-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wd5IAQAAIAAJ&q=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine |title=Impressions: Official Publication of the Ukiyo-e Society of America |date=2009 |publisher=Ukiyo-e Society of America |language=en}}</ref> formerly [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Farris |first=William Wayne |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SYOUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PA48 |title=A Bowl for a Coin: A Commodity History of Japanese Tea |date=2019 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8261-7 |language=en}}</ref> It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bronze Mirror of Suda Hachiman Jinja Shrine {{!}} Search Details |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R1-00643.html |access-date=2023-11-22 |website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=www.columbia.edu}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |jstor=42597980 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />

There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} '''Suda Hachiman Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bentley |first=John R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uaoLEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PT24 |title=The Birth of Japanese Historiography |date=2020-12-28 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-29569-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wd5IAQAAIAAJ&q=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine |title=Impressions: Official Publication of the Ukiyo-e Society of America |date=2009 |publisher=Ukiyo-e Society of America |language=en}}</ref> formerly [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Farris |first=William Wayne |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SYOUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PA48 |title=A Bowl for a Coin: A Commodity History of Japanese Tea |date=2019 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8261-7 |language=en}}</ref> It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bronze Mirror of Suda Hachiman Jinja Shrine {{!}} Search Details |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R1-00643.html |access-date=2023-11-22 |website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=www.columbia.edu}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |jstor=42597980 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />

Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |jstor=42597980 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} '''Suda Hachiman Shrine''' is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bentley |first=John R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uaoLEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PT24 |title=The Birth of Japanese Historiography |date=2020-12-28 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-29569-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wd5IAQAAIAAJ&q=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine |title=Impressions: Official Publication of the Ukiyo-e Society of America |date=2009 |publisher=Ukiyo-e Society of America |language=en}}</ref> formerly {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Farris |first=William Wayne |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SYOUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PA48 |title=A Bowl for a Coin: A Commodity History of Japanese Tea |date=2019 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8261-7 |language=en}}</ref> It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bronze Mirror of Suda Hachiman Jinja Shrine {{!}} Search Details |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R1-00643.html |access-date=2023-11-22 |website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=www.columbia.edu}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |jstor=42597980 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suda-hachiman-jinja]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine]][[ja:隅田八幡神社]][[ko:스다하치만 신사]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suda-hachiman-jinja]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine]][[ja:隅田八幡神社]][[ko:스다하치만 신사]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine]][[ja:隅田八幡神社]][[ko:스다하치만 신사]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=Suda-hachiman-jinja, haiden-2.jpg}} '''Suda Hachiman Shrine''' is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bentley |first=John R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uaoLEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PT24 |title=The Birth of Japanese Historiography |date=2020-12-28 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-29569-6 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wd5IAQAAIAAJ&q=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine |title=Impressions: Official Publication of the Ukiyo-e Society of America |date=2009 |publisher=Ukiyo-e Society of America |language=en}}</ref> formerly {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Farris |first=William Wayne |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SYOUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Suda+Hachiman+Shrine&pg=PA48 |title=A Bowl for a Coin: A Commodity History of Japanese Tea |date=2019 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8261-7 |language=en}}</ref> It was founded in 859. The [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] was found there. It is a National treasure of Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bronze Mirror of Suda Hachiman Jinja Shrine {{!}} Search Details |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R1-00643.html |access-date=2023-11-22 |website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Untitled |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~dbl11/Lurie-Suda-Mirror-Impressions.pdf |access-date=2023-10-17 |website=www.columbia.edu}}</ref> The Shrine is dedicated to [[Hachiman]].<ref>https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001561114.pdf</ref>
Suda Hachiman Shrine is located in Hashimoto City, Wakayama Prefecture, and stands as a significant historical and religious site in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Lurie |first=David Barnett |date=2009 |title=The Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror and Its Inscription |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42597980 |journal=Impressions |issue=30 |pages=27–31 |jstor=42597980 |issn=1095-2136}}</ref>
Established likely in the eleventh century, Suda Hachiman is a branch shrine of the prominent [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] situated in Kyoto. It was built on the Suda no Shõ estate.<ref name=":0" />
In 1355 it passed rules attempting to protect teapickers working on its land from labor abuses.<ref name=":1" />
The shrine is most renowned for its 6th-century cast bronze mirror, designated as a National Treasure of Japan. Measuring 19.8 cm in diameter, the mirror features a detailed design with nine human figures, one mounted on a horse. Its design is reminiscent of Chinese cast-bronze mirrors from the Later Han and Six Dynasties periods.<ref name=":0" />
This mirror's first recorded mention is in the 19th-century gazetteer ''Famous Places of Kii Province Illustrated'', serialized in Wakayama City between 1811 and 1851. Its origins are debated; some believe it was discovered during the Edo period (1615-1868), while others think it may have belonged to an older, pre-existing shrine.<ref name=":0" />
There are speculations regarding the mirror's origin. Some historians propose it was unearthed in the vicinity during the Edo period (1615-1868) along with other artifacts, while others theorize it could have belonged to an older shrine that was replaced by Suda Hachiman.<ref name=":0" />
